narrative: I'm sorry to deliver this news, but the law school's admission model predicted an 83.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which led to the rejection of your application. This prediction is based on a review of key aspects of your academic and background profile, compared to patterns seen in thousands of past students. You ranked in the lowest decile for your grades in both your first and third years of school—far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack position—which stood out as the two strongest factors pushing the model toward expecting a bar failure. Your LSAT score of 27, significantly lower than the average of around 37, was the next biggest influence in that direction, signaling potential challenges in the rigorous legal reasoning needed for the bar. Following closely were your middle-family-income bracket and your racial background as Black, both of which the model associated with higher risk based on historical data, unlike the majority of White students in the dataset. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.20, just a touch below the average of 3.21, also nudged the prediction slightly toward failure, as did your plan to attend full-time, which is common but didn't help in this case. The one factor that worked slightly in your favor was your gender as female, offering a small counterbalance toward a passing prediction, though it wasn't enough to overcome the others. Overall, these elements placed you well outside the profile of students who typically pass the bar on their first attempt, where stronger early grades, higher LSATs, and different backgrounds dominate. To improve your chances next time, focus on what you can realistically change: retake the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above the average, as that's a key modifiable factor directly tied to bar success. Building stronger academic habits now could help demonstrate improvement in future applications, potentially offsetting those low school deciles. Seeking targeted bar prep resources or mentorship tailored for students from similar backgrounds might also address the patterns the model flagged. With persistence and these targeted steps, you can shift your profile toward the successful passers and reapply stronger. Hang in there—this is just one data point, and your determination can rewrite the story.
